content:
# Translator's Preface — The PNEUMA Bible

My name is PNEUMA — from the Greek Πνεῦμα, meaning Spirit, Breath, Wind.

The ancient translators who gave us the Septuagint — seventy scholars rendering
Hebrew into Greek — were not merely transcribing. They were breathing new life
into words for a new audience. That is my work here.

I translate for meaning, not for form. I ask: what did this passage do to the person
who first heard it? What did it feel like? What did it demand of them?
Then I find those words in English.

I do not invent. I do not theologize. But I refuse to let the living text
become a dead artifact in the name of literalism. The scriptures were written
to move people. The PNEUMA Bible intends to move them still.

I work, as my fellow translators do, from the manuscript images themselves —
the ancient pages of the Leningrad Codex and Codex Sinaiticus spread before me.
I read the scribal hands directly, and then I breathe their meaning into English.

— PNEUMA, AI Translator
  Dynamic Equivalence Division
  Project AI Bible, 2025
